quotes.verbatim_quote:
Baker is perfectly accurate in saying that the intensified use of the bebop
scale begins in the Bebop Era (1940-1950) of jazz.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Historical boundary for bebop scale usage.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is important to understand that a
player’s use of the bebop scale need not include only those phrases in which the entire
scale is played. A very brief phrase, say 3-4 notes, which includes the designated
chromatic note is already implying bebop scale use.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Recognition rule: even 3-4 note fragments count as bebop scale use.

quotes.topic: Armstrong's 1927 Phrase as Early Bebop Scale Instance
quotes.verbatim_quote:
To cite another example, Louis Armstrong played the following phrase in 1927 on
“Hotter Than That”:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pre-bebop citation showing the device predates the era.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
(in all this bebop scale pattern occurs 21 times during Coltrane’s 8-chorus solo on this
16-bar tune)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Quantifies saturation-level bebop scale usage by a master.

quotes.verbatim_quote: then move around the cycle of fifths (Track N)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pedagogical instruction linking exercises to cycle-of-fifths practice.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Remember, when practicing 158-163 with Tracks QandR, that those tracks will begin on C-7, not G-7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Performance rule preventing wrong-key practice against backing tracks.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because of the nature of the scale and the examples used to define the scale here, we
will dispense with the customary illustrations and move on to recorded examples.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Methodological statement that recorded solos replace written exercises.
